<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490976 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00113: Introduction to the Alf API

- page_id: `170490976`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490976/Introduction+to+the+Alf+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:03:29.752+01:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Alf Plugin Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Alf Plugin provides a basic Application Programming Interface (API) that can be used to programmatically compile Alf code and import Alf files into a UML model. The Alf API classes are package in the *alf_api.jar* file located at:

- <MagicDraw installation directory>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.alf/alf_api.jar

You can also find JavaDoc for the Alf API at:

- <MagicDraw installation directory>/openapi/docs/alf/AlfJavaDoc.zip

For general information about creating a new MagicDraw plugin using Open APIs, see the MagicDraw [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modeling Tools Developer Guide' space='DEVG2024xR1'].

Before using the Alf Open API in your Java code, make sure that*alf-base.jar, alf.jar* and*alf_api.jar*have all been added to your IDE classpath.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compiler API' space='DEVG2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importer API' space='DEVG2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The Alf API classes are package in the <em>alf_api.jar</em> file located at:</p><ul><li><em>&lt;MagicDraw installation directory&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.alf/alf_api.jar</em></li></ul><p>You can also find JavaDoc for the Alf API at:</p><ul><li><em>&lt;MagicDraw installation directory&gt;/openapi/docs/alf/AlfJavaDoc.zip</em></li></ul><p>For general information about creating a new MagicDraw plugin using Open APIs, see the MagicDraw <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="Modeling Tools Developer Guide" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Developer Guide]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2330889b-cd1e-4182-bc87-162f0aeaf5b6"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Before using the Alf Open API in your Java code, make sure that </span><em>alf-base.jar, alf.jar</em> and<em> alf_api.jar</em><span> have all been added to your IDE classpath.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="97dbcebf-31e5-437e-a932-c13d87d59cf3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490874 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00114: INumberingAction interface

- page_id: `170490874`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490874/INumberingAction+interface
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-09T08:45:25.492+02:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Modeling Tools Developer Guide > Custom elements numbering
- labels: ['auto-number', 'numbering-scheme', 'number-part']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

What if we want to customize the numbering of elements even further? Let's say we need to number uml:Activities depending on their *getStructuredNode()*value. By implementing the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.INumberingAction* interface, it is possible to create the numbering that follow arbitrary conditions, this comes of course at the cost of increased complexity. The implementation must calculate the values for the numbers all by itself.

There are two parts to be explained that pertain to this interface. The actual interface that provides access to customized numbering and an immutable support class which encapsulates the values as defined in the *AutoNumber* and the associated *NumberingScheme* objects.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The *INumberingAction*Interface**. The interface has one method, which is invoked whenever a new *Element* is created or when **Create**or **Renumber**buttons are clicked in the **Element Numbering** dialog. This applies of course only if the Element type equals the type for which the *AutoNumber* has been customized. So at the time when a new element is created, this method receives a *java.util.List* with a single entry. On the other hand, when the user navigates the **Element Numbering**dialog, the method receives all elements that are currently listed in the right partition of the dialog. This implies that all elements have the same owner. Since this will give access to the complete Model Tree, it is possible to collect necessary information from other parts of the model and incorporate it. This method must return a mapping of each element together with the value that was generated for it. There is no need to number every element that the method receives. The Numbering Framework will register the generated values internally and set the property value for each element.

**The *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.NumberingInfo* support object.**This is an immutable Java Bean object which provides the implementer of the***INumberingAction* interface with all the information as defined in the *AutoNumber* and *NumberingScheme* objects:

- getName() returns the name of the AutoNumber .
- isDefaultNumber() returns the AutoNumber.defaultNumber value.
- getScheme() returns the AutoNumber.numberingScheme value, which is the NumberingScheme used to number this type of element.
- getProperty() returns the AutoNumber.numberedProperty value, which is the Property that will hold the value of the calculated number.
- getTarget() returns the AutoNumber.getTarget() value, which is the type of the elements to be numbered.
- isDisplayedProperty() returns true, if the value of the above getProperty() is displayed in the GUI. An element type can have more than one numbered Property , but only one can be displayed on symbols or in the Element Tree.
- getPrefix() returns the prefix as modified by the user in the Element Numbering dialog, which can be different from the AutoNumber.getPrefix() value (see the following figure).
- getSeparator() returns the separator as modified by the user in the Element Numbering dialog. In the implemented Numbering Framework, this value will override all the NumberParts of a type separator (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

For more information on how to create Numbering customizations with «AutoNumber», «NumberingScheme», and «NumberPart» elements, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with generic numbering mechanism' space='MD2024xR1'].

###### **Constraints**

A *NumberingScheme* that references an expression which implements this interface, can have one and only one *NumberPart*. Namely a *NumberPart* that references an *INumberingAction*.

###### **Example #1**

We shall now proceed to show a Java class that generates numbers for UML activities depending on their *getStructuredNode()* value. Since this class implements the***INumberingAction* interface, it should be set as the expression in the only *NumberPart* of this *NumberingScheme*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**

We have created a few activities inside a sample *Package*. With the creation of each new activity, an ID is generated and assigned to it. We then add some *StructuredActivityNodes* into two of the given activities. Now, in order to assign *Numbers* to these *Activities* that includes the number of *StructuredActivityNodes* children, we have to open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element Numbering dialog' space='MD2024xR1']**Element Numbering**dialog and renumber the Activities. This will generate new numbers that reflect the ownership relation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Let's have a look at the source code. This is a very simple example, which differentiates between receiving one or many elements, and calculates the values accordingly. This class can be found in *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.sample*.

```java
generateIds(List elements, NumberingInfo nInfo) {
            Map idMap = new HashMap();
            if (! elements.isEmpty()) {
 
                // the NumberingInfo object contains the values set in the customization
                String baseId = nInfo.getPrefix() + nInfo.getSeparator();
 
                // when creating an Activity, this will be called with a single element 
                if (elements.size() == 1) {
                    Element e = elements.iterator().next();
                    Class>[] types = new Class>[]{Activity.class};
                    Collection extends Element> acts = ModelHelper.getElementsOfType(e.getOwner(), types, false, true);
                    String id = baseId + (acts.size());
                    idMap.put(e, id);

 
                    // when renumbering in the dialog this will be called with multiple elements
                } else {
                    int counter = 1;
 
                    // we sort so that renumber will get the same order
                    // normally this would ask for a custom comparator class
                    // but it is left out for brevity's sake
                    Collections.sort(elements);
                    for (Element e : elements) {
                        if (e instanceof Activity) { 
                            Activity act = (Activity) e; 
                            String id = baseId + counter; 
                            counter ++;
                            Collection nodes = act.getStructuredNode();
 
                            // we simply attach the suffix '$node_' and the node count 
                            if (nodes != null && !nodes.isEmpty()) { 
                                id += "$node_" + nodes.size();
                            }
                            idMap.put(e, id);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
            // returning the mapping of elements and ids, so that the framework can register them
            return idMap;
        }
    }]]>
```

###### **Example #2**

A more complicated example can be found the OpenAPI packages *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid* and *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custo*. It shows an implementation that creates numbers for elements connected through UML Relationships. The UML diagram shows the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custom.DirectedAssociationNumbering* class, which is customized to number element types that are connected by «DirectedAssociation» links.

The interested Reader should consult the source code, in order to learn how such an algorithm has been implemented.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

*Example of relationship numbering (fragment)*

**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>What if we want to customize the numbering of elements even further? Let's say we need to number uml:Activities depending on their <em>getStructuredNode() </em>value. By implementing the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.INumberingAction</em> interface, it is possible to create the numbering that follow arbitrary conditions, this comes of course at the cost of increased complexity. The implementation must calculate the values for the numbers all by itself.</p><p>There are two parts to be explained that pertain to this interface. The actual interface that provides access to customized numbering and an immutable support class which encapsulates the values as defined in the <em>AutoNumber</em> and the associated <em>NumberingScheme</em> objects.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_of_Numberingaction_interface_and_numberingInfo_support_object.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><div class="WordSection1"><p><strong>The <em>INumberingAction </em>Interface</strong>. The interface has one method, which is invoked whenever a new <em>Element</em> is created or when <strong>Create </strong>or <strong>Renumber </strong>buttons are clicked in the <strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog. This applies of course only if the Element type equals the type for which the <em>AutoNumber</em> has been customized. So at the time when a new element is created, this method receives a <em>java.util.List</em> with a single entry. On the other hand, when the user navigates the <strong>Element Numbering </strong>dialog, the method receives all elements that are currently listed in the right partition of the dialog. This implies that all elements have the same owner. Since this will give access to the complete Model Tree, it is possible to collect necessary information from other parts of the model and incorporate it. This method must return a mapping of each element together with the value that was generated for it. There is no need to number every element that the method receives. The Numbering Framework will register the generated values internally and set the property value for each element.</p><p><strong>The <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.NumberingInfo</em> support object. </strong>This is an immutable Java Bean object which provides the implementer of the<em> </em><em>INumberingAction</em> interface with all the information as defined in the <em>AutoNumber</em> and <em>NumberingScheme</em> objects:</p><ul><li><em>getName()</em> returns the name of the <em>AutoNumber</em>.</li><li><em>isDefaultNumber()</em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.defaultNumber</em> value.</li><li><em>getScheme()</em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.numberingScheme</em> value, which is the <em>NumberingScheme</em> used to number this type of element.</li><li><em>getProperty()</em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.numberedProperty</em> value, which is the <em>Property</em> that will hold the value of the calculated number.</li><li><em>getTarget()</em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.getTarget()</em> value, which is the type of the elements to be numbered.</li><li><em>isDisplayedProperty()</em> returns true, if the value of the above <em>getProperty()</em> is displayed in the GUI. An element type can have more than one numbered <em>Property</em>, but only one can be displayed on symbols or in the Element Tree.</li><li><em>getPrefix()</em> returns the prefix as modified by the user in the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Element Numbering </strong>dialog, which can be different from the <em>AutoNumber.getPrefix()</em> value (see the following figure).</li><li><em>getSeparator()</em> returns the separator as modified by the user in the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Element Numbering </strong>dialog. In the implemented Numbering Framework, this value will override all the <em>NumberParts</em> of a type separator (see the following figure).</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:title="Modified Separator and Prefix values (fragment of Element Numbering dialog)" ac:alt="Modified Separator and Prefix values (fragment of Element Numbering dialog)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="modified_separator_and_prefix_values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>For more information on how to create Numbering customizations with «AutoNumber», «NumberingScheme», and «NumberPart» elements, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with generic numbering mechanism" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Working with Generic Numbering Mechanism]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></p><h4><strong>Constraints</strong></h4><p>A <em>NumberingScheme</em> that references an expression which implements this interface, can have one and only one <em>NumberPart</em>. Namely a <em>NumberPart</em> that references an <em>INumberingAction</em>.</p><h4><strong>Example #1</strong></h4><p>We shall now proceed to show a Java class that generates numbers for UML activities depending on their <em>getStructuredNode()</em> value. Since this class implements the<em> </em><em>INumberingAction</em> interface, it should be set as the expression in the only <em>NumberPart</em> of this <em>NumberingScheme</em>.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="ActivityScheme numbering scheme (fragment)" ac:alt="ActivityScheme numbering scheme (fragment)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="activityScheme_numbering_scheme.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><em><br /></em></p><div class="WordSection1"><p>We have created a few activities inside a sample <em>Package</em>. With the creation of each new activity, an ID is generated and assigned to it. We then add some <em>StructuredActivityNodes</em> into two of the given activities. Now, in order to assign <em>Numbers</em> to these <em>Activities</em> that includes the number of <em>StructuredActivityNodes</em> children, we have to open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Element Numbering </strong>dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> and renumber the Activities. This will generate new numbers that reflect the ownership relation.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Activities and Structural Nodes before and after renumbering using Element Numbering dialog" ac:alt="Activities and Structural Nodes before and after renumbering using Element Numbering dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Activities and Structural Nodes before and after renumbering using Element Numbering dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></div><div class="WordSection1"><p><br /></p><p>Let's have a look at the source code. This is a very simple example, which differentiates between receiving one or many elements, and calculates the values accordingly. This class can be found in <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.sample</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b687e352-9cd5-43f5-80d6-572301180f43"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    public class ActivityNodeNumbering implements INumberingAction {
        @Override
        public Map<Element, String> generateIds(List<Element> elements, NumberingInfo nInfo) {
            Map<Element, String> idMap = new HashMap<Element, String>();
            if (! elements.isEmpty()) {
 
                // the NumberingInfo object contains the values set in the customization
                String baseId = nInfo.getPrefix() + nInfo.getSeparator();
 
                // when creating an Activity, this will be called with a single element 
                if (elements.size() == 1) {
                    Element e = elements.iterator().next();
                    Class<?>[] types = new Class<?>[]{Activity.class};
                    Collection<? extends Element> acts = ModelHelper.getElementsOfType(e.getOwner(), types, false, true);
                    String id = baseId + (acts.size());
                    idMap.put(e, id);

 
                    // when renumbering in the dialog this will be called with multiple elements
                } else {
                    int counter = 1;
 
                    // we sort so that renumber will get the same order
                    // normally this would ask for a custom comparator class
                    // but it is left out for brevity's sake
                    Collections.sort(elements);
                    for (Element e : elements) {
                        if (e instanceof Activity) { 
                            Activity act = (Activity) e; 
                            String id = baseId + counter; 
                            counter ++;
                            Collection<StructuredActivityNode> nodes = act.getStructuredNode();
 
                            // we simply attach the suffix '$node_' and the node count 
                            if (nodes != null && !nodes.isEmpty()) { 
                                id += "$node_" + nodes.size();
                            }
                            idMap.put(e, id);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
            // returning the mapping of elements and ids, so that the framework can register them
            return idMap;
        }
    }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div><h4><strong>Example #2</strong></h4><p>A more complicated example can be found the OpenAPI packages <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid</em> and <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custo</em>. It shows an implementation that creates numbers for elements connected through UML Relationships. The UML diagram shows the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custom.DirectedAssociationNumbering</em> class, which is customized to number element types that are connected by «DirectedAssociation» links.</p><p>The interested Reader should consult the source code, in order to learn how such an algorithm has been implemented.</p><p><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_of_relationship_numbering.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><em>Example of relationship numbering (fragment)</em></p><p><em><br /></em></p></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

| Diagram palette |  |
| Diagram toolbar |  |
| Browser shortcut menu |  |
| Diagram shortcut menu |  |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490938 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00116: Java APIs

- page_id: `170490938`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490938/Java+APIs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:55:27.498+02:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Cameo Simulation Toolkit Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

791502930

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

791502932

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

791502931

Through Java APIs, a simulation execution starts when the specific element is executed. You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Starting execution' space='DEVG2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stopping execution' space='DEVG2024xR1'], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and registering a new SimulationExecutionListener' space='DEVG2024xR1'] using Java APIs. Regarding the core component of the entire execution mechanism, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Engine' space='DEVG2024xR1'] defines how to execute a set of Element types. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='fUML Helper' space='DEVG2024xR1'] is also available as a Class that provides Helper methods related to the fUML structures. The Parametric engine also provides Java APIs for a parametric execution with a runtime object of a Classifier.

#### INFO: Information

Information

For more information about Cameo Simulation Toolkit JavaDoc files, visit [https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024xRefresh1/com/nomagic/magicdraw/simulation/package-summary.html](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024xRefresh1/com/nomagic/magicdraw/simulation/package-summary.html).

791502929

**Related pages**

The Parametric engine also provides Java APIs for a parametric execution with a runtime object of a Classifier.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ff59532-b07a-49de-8b7d-553d3f24f7e5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information about Cameo Simulation Toolkit JavaDoc files, visit <a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024xRefresh1/com/nomagic/magicdraw/simulation/package-summary.html">https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024xRefresh1/com/nomagic/magicdraw/simulation/package-summary.html</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c7a755d7-e23f-4385-9a20-0c9ac57cb167"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">791502929</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="350125f7-b1e4-43ad-b7cd-09284b21119b" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490906 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00117: JavaScript migration from Nashorn to Rhino engine

- page_id: `170490906`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490906/JavaScript+migration+from+Nashorn+to+Rhino+engine
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-12-08T15:24:36.411+01:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Modeling Tools Developer Guide
- labels: ['js', 'javascript', 'nashorn', 'rhino']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The JavaScript *Nashorn* has been removed from the modeling tool ([learn more about deprecated JavaScript Nashorn](https://openjdk.java.net/jeps/335)). Please use JavaScript *Rhino* instead.

To find the usages of the JavaScript *Nashorn* in the project, run the**Deprecated JavaScript**validation suite (**Analyze**>**Validation**>**Validate**).

The key differences are listed below. For more information, please check [Nashorn/Rhino Migration Guide](https://wiki.openjdk.java.net/display/Nashorn/Rhino+Migration+Guide).

###### Class object and .class property

If a java API accepts a `java.lang.Class` object, in *Nashorn* "`.class`" property (similar to Java) is used. In *Rhino* you pass script representation of class "as is".

```js

```

###### Accessing Java packages and classes from script

*Nashorn*'s recommended way to access Java classes (by using `Java.type`) is not supported in *Rhino*.

```js

```

###### Creating Java arrays from script

In *Nashorn*, you can resolve to a Java array class using the same `Java.type` API. And array creation is done using `new` operator. In *Rhino*, you create a Java array using Java reflection from script.

```js

```

###### Java exceptions

In *Nashorn* Java exception objects are thrown "as is". *Rhino* wraps Java exceptions as a script object. If you want underlying Java exception, use "`javaException`" property to access it.

```js
} 
catch (e)
{
    // Nashorn
    e.printStackTrace()
    // Rhino
    e.javaException.printStackTrace()
}]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> has been removed from the modeling tool (<a href="https://openjdk.java.net/jeps/335" class="external-link" rel="nofollow">learn more about deprecated JavaScript Nashorn</a>). Please use JavaScript <em>Rhino</em> instead.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="741ddd1a-e9e5-44f7-bd08-f5442b90bb69"><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span>To find the usages of the JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> in the project, run the </span><strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong><span> validation suite (</span><strong>Analyze</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Validation</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Validate</strong><span>).</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>The key differences are listed below. For more information, please check <a href="https://wiki.openjdk.java.net/display/Nashorn/Rhino+Migration+Guide">Nashorn/Rhino Migration Guide</a>.</span></p><h4>Class object and .class property</h4><p>If a java API accepts a <code>java.lang.Class</code> object, in <em>Nashorn</em> &quot;<code>.class</code>&quot; property (similar to Java) is used. In <em>Rhino</em> you pass script representation of class &quot;as is&quot;.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de79df2b-dc41-4e29-96a3-c977d53eb9e8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">js</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[// Nashorn
var types = [com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Class.class]
// Rhino
var types = [com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Class]

var project = com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Application.getInstance().getProject()
var Finder = com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.Finder
Finder.byTypeRecursively().find(project, types, false)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4><span>Accessing Java packages and classes from script</span></h4><p><span><em>Nashorn</em>'s recommended way to access Java classes (by using <code>Java.type</code>) is not supported in <em>Rhino</em>.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa704280-2718-408e-a95a-6ecebdd8c756"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">js</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[// Nashorn
var Vector = Java.type("java.util.Vector")
var JFrame = Java.type("javax.swing.JFrame")

// Rhino
var Vector = java.util.Vector
var JFrame = Packages.javax.swing.JFrame]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Creating Java arrays from script</h4><p>In <em>Nashorn</em>, you can resolve to a Java array class using the same <code>Java.type</code> API. And array creation is done using <code>new</code> operator. In <em>Rhino</em>, you create a Java array using Java reflection from script.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cd4ea3e8-83a4-47ce-b902-ffd3ab4285b9"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">js</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[// Nashorn
var IntArray = Java.type("int[]")
var array = new IntArray(8)

// Rhino
var Array = java.lang.reflect.Array
var intClass = java.lang.Integer.TYPE
var array = Array.newInstance(intClass, 8)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Java exceptions</h4><p>In <em>Nashorn</em> Java exception objects are thrown &quot;as is&quot;. <em>Rhino</em> wraps Java exceptions as a script object. If you want underlying Java exception, use &quot;<code>javaException</code>&quot; property to access it.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6221a914-ad37-4036-a565-aec168c5a659"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">js</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[try
{
    <...>
} 
catch (e)
{
    // Nashorn
    e.printStackTrace()
    // Rhino
    e.javaException.printStackTrace()
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490902 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00118: Jython Scripting

- page_id: `170490902`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490902/Jython+Scripting
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T16:08:52.013+01:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Modeling Tools Developer Guide
- labels: ['jython', 'python']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

679375408

679375410

679375409

Modeling tools allow you to access open API using the Jython scripting language.

Jython is an implementation of the high-level, dynamic, and object-oriented language Python which is seamlessly integrated with the Java platform.

Using Jython you may access all java API and MagicDraw (or other modeling tool developed by us) open API. This allows to avoid compilation and to get the same results without a java code. Using scripting you may do everything that you can achieve using a java plugin, and even more: you may change your code without recompiling and restarting an application.

More information about Jython you can find at [http://www.jython.org](http://www.jython.org/). Information about the python language you can find at [http://www.python.org](http://www.python.org/).

On every startup, program checks for scripts in *plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.jpython/scripts/*. If there are subdirectories, each of them are scanned for the *script.xml*file. This file provides information about the script in this directory. A file is similar to a plugin descriptor described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Plugin descriptor' space='DEVG2024xR1']. If *script.xml*contains valid information, the script file specified in *script.xml*is executed. The script file should contain a valid Jython 2.7.2 script.

679375407

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f934e4e4-78cf-4619-80ce-1a4422fb4630"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">679375408</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d0a39947-c95a-40d1-a313-174615c5c86e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">679375410</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfd54cca-360e-40be-ab55-920c6f368cc5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">679375409</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Modeling tools allow you to access open API using the Jython scripting language.</p><p>Jython is an implementation of the high-level, dynamic, and object-oriented language Python which is seamlessly integrated with the Java platform.</p><p>Using Jython you may access all java API and MagicDraw (or other modeling tool developed by us) open API. This allows to avoid compilation and to get the same results without a java code. Using scripting you may do everything that you can achieve using a java plugin, and even more: you may change your code without recompiling and restarting an application.</p><p>More information about Jython you can find at <a style="line-height: 1.4285715;" href="http://www.jython.org/"><u>http://ww</u><u>w</u><u>.jython.org</u></a>. Information about the python language you can find at <a href="http://www.python.org/" style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><u>http://ww</u><u>w</u><u>.python.org</u></a>.</p><p>On every startup, program checks for scripts in <em>plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.jpython/scripts/</em>. If there are subdirectories, each of them are scanned for the <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">script.xml </em>file. This file provides information about the script in this directory. A file is similar to a plugin descriptor described in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="Plugin descriptor" /></ac:link>. If <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">script.xml </em>contains valid information, the script file specified in <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">script.xml </em>is executed. The script file should contain a valid Jython 2.7.2 script.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33009826-4d09-48d2-93ce-3249dc19e1ee"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">679375407</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="da9215b1-463e-4299-9093-1f4e3b460f7c" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490792 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00119: Layouting symbols in the diagram

- page_id: `170490792`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490792/Layouting+symbols+in+the+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-13T10:21:45.854+01:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Modeling Tools Developer Guide > Working with diagrams > Presentation elements (symbols) > Working with presentation elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

We provide an API to layout presentation elements in a diagram using different strategies (layouter).

- com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.DiagramPresentationElement.layout(boolean)
- DiagramPresentationElement.layout(boolean, DiagramLayouter[)](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/uml/symbols/DiagramPresentationElement.html#layout-boolean-com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.layout.DiagramLayouter-)
- DiagramPresentationElement.layout(boolean, DiagramLayouter[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/uml/symbols/DiagramPresentationElement.html#layout-boolean-com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.layout.DiagramLayouter-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.options.AbstractDiagramLayouterOptionsGroup-)AbstractDiagramLayouterOptionsGroup[)](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/uml/symbols/DiagramPresentationElement.html#layout-boolean-com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.layout.DiagramLayouter-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.options.AbstractDiagramLayouterOptionsGroup-)

Make sure the diagram is opened and the session is closed before doing a diagram layout. These are the current limitation of layout API

****You can find the code examples in

- <installation_directory>\openapi\examples\testframework, com.nomagic.magicdraw.examples.testframework.DiagramLayoutTest class

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Session management' space='DEVG2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490972 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00120: LDAP configuration description

- page_id: `170490972`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490972/LDAP+configuration+description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-12-08T15:27:56.945+01:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Teamwork Cloud Developer Guide > REST APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following table describes the key-values for changing the LDAP configuration via REST API:

| Key-value | Description |
| --- | --- |
| "java.naming.factory.initial" | An optional key-value describing the initial context factory to be used, such as "com.sun.jndi.ldap.LdapCtxFactory". |
| "com.sun.jndi.ldap.read.timeout" | The maximum amount of time in milliseconds for an LDAP request or a read timeout, e.g., "10000" |
| "weight" | An optional LDAP weight value used to order the LDAP realm for authentication, such as "1", "2", "3". |
| "enabled" | The key-value describing if the LDAP realm is enabled ("true") or disabled ("false"). |
| "com.sun.jndi.ldap.connect.timeout" | The maximum amount of time in milliseconds for the LDAP provider to establish connection, e.g., "5000". If connection is not established within a timeout period, it is aborted. |
| "searchbase" | The starting point of the search in the LDAP directory tree, such as "dc=example,dc=com". |
| "query" | The LDAP search filter value for finding, retrieving, and importing users (used when "authen_dntype" : "query"). The value depends on the LDAP server, e.g., "(uid={0})" or "(&(cn={0})(objectClass=user))". |
| "usergroup_query" | The LDAP search filter value for finding, retrieving, and importing user groups. The value depends on the LDAP server, e.g., "(cn={0})" or "(&(cn={0})(objectClass=group))". |
| "authen_dntype" | The authentication type value. Use the "template" value when one-level search can be used to login. Use the "query" value when sub-level search can be used to login. |
| "userDNTemplate" | The user template value used to search for users by a specific user path in one-level scope. The user name in the user path will be "{0}", e.g., "uid={0},dc=example,dc=com". |
| "anoymousbind" | The anonymous binding allows to connect and search without logging in to the LDAP server. Normally, the value is "false". This value can be used only when the LDAP server allows it. |
| "ldap_realm_name" | The name of the LDAP realm, such as "Apache LDAP" or "AD1". |
| "java.naming.security.protocol" | The protocol for connecting to the LDAP server, such as "ssl" or "none". |
| "key_cer_file_content" | The SSL key file content. If "java.naming.security.protocol" is "none", the value can be "". |
| "protocol" | The LDAP protocol, e.g., "ldap". |
| "port" | The LDAP port, e.g., "10389". |
| "IP" | The server IP address, e.g., "127.0.0.1". |
| "ID" | The ID of the LDAP realm. This value is generated from the Teamwork Cloud server. |
| "authen" | The authentication type. Use the "simple" value for a clear-text password. Use the "sasl_mech" value for a space-separated list of the SASL mechanism names. You can use one of the SASL mechanisms, e.g., "CRAM-MD5" which means that the CRAM-MD5 SASL mechanism described in RFC 2195 will be used. |
| "userName" | The user name used to log in to the LDAP server, such as "uid=admin,ou=system". |
| "password" | The password used to log in to the LDAP server. |
| "url" | The URL constructed from "IP", "port", and "protocol", e.g., "ldap://127.0.0.1:10389". |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The following table describes the key-values for changing the LDAP configuration via REST API:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 65.9239%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 18.8944%;" /><col style="width: 81.1056%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><strong>Key-value</strong></th><th><strong>Description</strong></th></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;java.naming.factory.initial&quot;</strong></td><td><span>An optional key-value describing the initial context factory to be used, such as &quot;com.sun.jndi.ldap.LdapCtxFactory&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;com.sun.jndi.ldap.read.timeout&quot;</strong></td><td><p><span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The maximum amount of time in milliseconds for an LDAP request or a read timeout, e.g., <span>&quot;10000&quot;</span></span></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;weight&quot;</strong></td><td><span>An optional LDAP weight value used to order the LDAP realm for authentication, such as &quot;1&quot;, &quot;2&quot;, &quot;3&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;enabled&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The key-value describing if the LDAP realm is enabled (&quot;true&quot;) or disabled (&quot;false&quot;).</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;com.sun.jndi.ldap.connect.timeout&quot;</strong></td><td><p><span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The maximum amount of time in milliseconds for the <span>LDAP provider to establish connection, e.g., <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">&quot;5000&quot;</span>. If connection is not established within a timeout period, it is aborted.</span></span></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;searchbase&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The starting point of the search in the LDAP directory tree, such as &quot;dc=example,dc=com&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;query&quot;</strong></td><td><p>The LDAP search filter value for finding<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">, retrieving, and importing users</span> (used when &quot;authen_dntype&quot; : &quot;query&quot;). The value depends on the LDAP server, e.g., &quot;(uid={0})&quot; or &quot;(&amp;(cn={0})(objectClass=user))&quot;.</p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;usergroup_query&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The LDAP search filter value for<span> finding</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">, retrieving, and importing </span>user groups. The value depends on the LDAP server, e.g., &quot;(cn={0})&quot; or &quot;(&amp;(cn={0})(objectClass=group))&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;authen_dntype&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The authentication type value. Use the <span>&quot;template&quot; value</span> <span>when one-level search can be used to login. Use the &quot;query&quot; value <span>when sub-level search can be used to login.</span> </span></span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;userDNTemplate&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The user template value used to search for users by a specific user path <span>in</span><span> one-level scope</span>. The user name in the user path will be &quot;{0}&quot;, e.g., &quot;uid={0},dc=example,dc=com&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;anoymousbind&quot;</strong></td><td><span><span>The anonymous binding allows to connect and search without logging in to the LDAP server. Normally, the value is &quot;false&quot;. </span>This value can be used only when the LDAP server allows it.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;ldap_realm_name&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The name of the LDAP realm, such as &quot;Apache LDAP&quot; or &quot;AD1&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;java.naming.security.protocol&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The protocol for connecting to the LDAP server, such as &quot;ssl&quot; or &quot;none&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;key_cer_file_content&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The SSL key file content. If &quot;java.naming.security.protocol&quot; is &quot;none&quot;, the value can be &quot;&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td><strong>&quot;protocol&quot;</strong></td><td><span>The LDAP protocol, e.g., &quot;ldap&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;port&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>The LDAP port, e.g., &quot;10389&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;IP&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>The server IP address, e.g., &quot;127.0.0.1&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;ID&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>The ID of the LDAP realm. This value is generated from the Teamwork Cloud server.</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;authen&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>The authentication type. Use the &quot;simple&quot; value for a clear-text password. Use the &quot;<span>sasl_mech&quot; value for a<span> space-separated list of the SASL mechanism names. <span>You can use one of the SASL mechanisms, e.g., &quot;CRAM-MD5&quot; which means that the CRAM-MD5 SASL mechanism described in RFC 2195 will be used.</span></span></span></span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;userName&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>The user name used to log in to the LDAP server, such as &quot;uid=admin,ou=system&quot;.</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;password&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1">The password used to log in to the LDAP server.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>&quot;url&quot;</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>The URL constructed from &quot;IP&quot;, &quot;port&quot;, and &quot;protocol&quot;, e.g., &quot;<a href="ldap://127.0.0.1:10389">ldap://127.0.0.1:10389</a>&quot;.</span></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170490922 space=DEVG2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00121: Licensing information

- page_id: `170490922`
- space_key: `DEVG2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG2024xR1/pages/170490922/Licensing+information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-13T09:54:49.295+01:00`
- ancestors: Developer Guides Home > Modeling Tools Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

513480473

INLINE

513480494

INLINE

513480484

INLINE

##### Providing licensing information

It is possible to provide the tool with the licensing information using the Java (JVM) system properties.

You can specify either seat or floating license.

- Seat license (license file) properties:

| Property | Value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| LICENSE_FILE_FORCE_USAGE | true | when specified, the tool tries using the license file specified in LICENSE_FILE property. Allows changing the license. |
| LICENSE_FILE | path to the license file or directory with the license files | when the tool has no license specified yet, or LICENSE_FILE_FORCE_USAGE is specified, the tool uses a license specified by LICENSE_FILE property.Surround the path with double quotes if it contains spaces. |

- Floating license (FlexNet license server) properties:

| Property | Value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| FL_FORCE_USAGE | true | when specified, the tool uses a floating license. |
| FL_SERVER_ADDRESS | FlexNet license server name or IP address |  |
| FL_SERVER_PORT | FlexNet license server port number |  |
| FL_EDITION | tool edition | specify if the tool supports editions; otherwise, omit. |

`-D` must be prepended to the property name if the Java system property is specified through java command-line arguments/parameters.

E.g., in the tool launcher properties file (`bin/<launcher name>.properties`), add floating license properties:

`JAVA_ARGS=<...> -DFL_FORCE_USAGE\=true -DFL_SERVER_ADDRESS\=myServer -DFL_SERVER_PORT\=1101 -DFL_EDITION\=Enterprise`

or specify license file:

`JAVA_ARGS=<...> -DLICENSE_FILE\="c:/my licenses/licenseFile.txt"`

##### Getting licensing information

Use the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.license.utils.LicenseUtils* to check some licensing information (edition, is it a trial or evaluation version).

513480472

INLINE

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Configure test environment' space='DEVG2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying batch mode program classpath and required system properties' space='DEVG2024xR1']
